2: ou gar ekrina ti eidenai en hymin ei me Iesoun Christon kai touton estauromemon: 'For I decided to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and him crucified.' Ekrina is deliberate resolve, not ignorance. Paul came to the most philosophically sophisticated city in Greece and chose (krinō) to deploy only one argument: the cross. Estauromenos (crucified) is the perfect passive participle — not 'who was crucified' (past and done) but 'who is the crucified one' (ongoing identity). Paul's Christology is permanently cross-shaped.
13: pneumatikois pneumatika synkrinontes (pneumatikois pneumatika synkrinotes): 'interpreting spiritual things to spiritual people' (or 'comparing spiritual things with spiritual things'). The phrase is syntactically ambiguous: synkrinō can mean 'combine' (matching Spirit-words with Spirit-people) or 'interpret' (explaining spiritual realities). Either way, Paul's point is that the Spirit's deep things (1 Corinthians 2:10: ta bathe tou theou) are disclosed only within the Spirit's own interpretive community — those who have the mind of Christ (1 Corinthians 2:16).
1: κατὰ ὑπεροχὴν λόγου ἢ σοφίας (with superiority of speech or wisdom) — the prepositional phrase kata hyperochen (according to pre-eminence/superiority) describes the rhetorical mode Paul deliberately did not use. Some manuscripts read μυστήριον (mystery) for μαρτύριον (testimony) — if mystērion is original, it introduces the hidden-wisdom theme from the start; if martyrion, the focus is on testimony/proclamation. The UBS/NA reading martyrion is followed here, but the variant is ancient and substantive.
3: ἐγὼ ἐν ἀσθενείᾳ καὶ ἐν φόβῳ καὶ ἐν τρόμῳ πολλῷ (I in weakness and in fear and in much trembling) — the triple prepositional phrase (en... kai en... kai en...) with emphatic pollō (much) describes Paul's actual experiential state in Corinth. This is not false modesty; it is the self-conscious acknowledgment that the divine power operates through a broken vessel. The contrast with the Corinthian puffed-up wisdom sets the apostolic paradigm: weakness, not strength, is the proper medium for divine power (cf. 2 Corinthians 12:9-10).
4: ἀπόδειξις πνεύματος καὶ δυνάμεως (demonstration of the Spirit and power) — apodeixis is a technical rhetorical/philosophical term for logical demonstration or proof, the strongest form of argument. Paul claims the Spirit provides a different kind of apodeixis than rhetoric does — not logical persuasion but direct conviction through the Spirit's power. The genitive pneumatos (of the Spirit) makes the Spirit the mode and content of this demonstration, not its audience.
5: ἵνα ἡ πίστις ὑμῶν μὴ ᾖ ἐν σοφίᾳ ἀνθρώπων ἀλλ᾽ ἐν δυνάμει θεοῦ (so that your faith might not be in human wisdom but in God's power) — purpose clause with present subjunctive: the ongoing character of the faith-stance matters, not just its origin. Faith resting on rhetorical persuasion would be as fragile as the arguer's skill; faith resting on God's power rests on something that cannot be refuted by a better rhetorician.
6: τελείοις (teleiois = the mature, the complete) — the word was used in Hellenistic mystery religions for the fully initiated, and in philosophical schools for the advanced student. Paul's use is deliberately counter-cultural: he does speak wisdom, but to the teleioi; the irony is that the Corinthians who claim this status are proved by their divisions to be nēpioi (infants, 1 Corinthians 3:1). The wisdom Paul offers the mature is NOT the wisdom of this age.
7: ἐν μυστηρίῳ τὴν ἀποκεκρυμμένην (in mystery, the having-been-hidden-one) — the perfect passive participle apokekrimmēnēn (from apokryptō, to hide thoroughly) emphasizes the completeness of the prior hiddenness. This wisdom was not merely obscure but thoroughly concealed before God revealed it. The perfect tense insists that the prior hiddenness is the necessary context for understanding the magnitude of the revelation.
8: τὸν κύριον τῆς δόξης (the Lord of glory) — an unusual christological title. In the LXX and Jewish apocalyptic literature (1 Enoch 1 Corinthians 22:14; 1 Corinthians 25:3; 1 Corinthians 27:3; 1 Corinthians 36:4; Psalms 24:7-10), Kyrios tēs doxēs is a title for YHWH as the glorious king of the cosmos. Paul applies it to Jesus as the one the rulers of this age crucified — an implicit argument for divine identity. The rulers' ignorance of whom they crucified is matched by the title's claim about who he was.
9: ἃ ὀφθαλμὸς οὐκ εἶδεν (what eye has not seen) — Paul introduces a free citation of Isaiah 64:4 and possibly 65:17 with alla kathōs gegraptai (but as it stands written). The emphasis is on the radical incommensurability of God's prepared blessings with any human investigative framework: not seen, not heard, not risen up into the heart of a human being. The three negated faculties (sight, hearing, understanding) exhaust the channels of normal human cognition.
10: ἡμῖν δὲ ἀπεκάλυψεν ὁ θεὸς διὰ τοῦ πνεύματος (but to us God revealed through the Spirit) — aorist active: a completed divine disclosure. Πνεῦμα (pneuma) here carries its highest dispute load (G4151, dispute_level 3): it is Spirit (divine power) not merely wind, and specifically the Holy Spirit as an agent of revelation, not merely an inspirational force. Eraunaō (searches) in the present tense: the Spirit's investigation of the depths of God (ta bathē tou theou) is ongoing and penetrating.
11: τίς γὰρ οἶδεν ἀνθρώπων τὰ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου (for who of people knows the things of a person) — the analogy moves from the human level to the divine: just as only a person's own spirit knows that person's inner thoughts, so only God's Spirit knows God's depths. The analogy grounds the Spirit's cognitive access to the divine mind in a recognizable epistemology. Πνεῦμα used twice: human spirit (the person's own consciousness) and the Spirit of God — different referents in parallel positions.
12: ἐλάβομεν (we received) — aorist: a completed act of reception. The Spirit received is specifically οὐ τὸ πνεῦμα τοῦ κόσμου (not the spirit of the world) — the articular noun construction (the spirit of the world vs. the Spirit from God) makes these two definite, distinct realities. Ἵνα εἰδῶμεν (so that we might know) — purpose: Spirit-reception has a specific cognitive goal: knowing what God has freely given, not experiencing ecstasy or demonstrating power.
14: ψυχικὸς ἄνθρωπος (the natural/soulish person) — psychikos is the adjective from psychē (soul, the animating life-principle). The natural person operates at the level of the psychē alone, without the Spirit. Paul's anthropological categories are not body/soul/spirit in a Platonic hierarchy but rather Spirit-equipped vs. Spirit-lacking — a pneumatological distinction, not a metaphysical one. Μωρία (foolishness) recurs from 1 Corinthians 1:18-25: the natural mind's verdict on Spirit-matters is the same as the cross-rejector's verdict on the gospel.
15: ὁ δὲ πνευματικὸς ἀνακρίνει μὲν πάντα (the spiritual person discerns all things) — anakrinō is a legal-interrogative term: to examine, to cross-examine, to evaluate after investigation. The spiritual person has the capacity to subject all things to discerning examination — not omniscience but Spirit-enabled discernment. The passive counterpart: autos de hyp' oudenos anakrinetai — but he himself is examined by no one (oudeis = no human authority can render a binding verdict on him, since his life is accountable to God alone).
16: νοῦν κυρίου (mind of the Lord) — Paul cites Isaiah 40:13 (LXX: tis egnō noun kyriou? — who knew the mind of the Lord?) to establish that unequipped humanity cannot know God's mind. Then the jarring counter: ἡμεῖς δὲ νοῦν Χριστοῦ ἔχομεν (but we have the mind of Christ). The substitution of Christou (Christ's) for kyriou (Lord's) is a divine-identity claim: having the mind of Christ is having the mind of YHWH. This is the climax of the entire wisdom-Spirit argument of chapters 1-2.
